commentBody: I never expected to say this, but this is the first time I ever found a Friday puzzle easier than most weekdays. A new personal best, by a long shot, too. I hope it means I was just in sync with this one.
Interesting puzzle. The top third was difficult for me but after finishing the bottom 2/3rds it came together pretty easily. Enjoyable solve! Maybe could have been a skosh more challenging.

That was not my fastest NYT Friday puzzle, but it felt almost as if it was. Working on the short verticals got enough letters to guess the long 15s — all nine of them — and I was left trying to figure out why S _ _ _ P was a trap. Aha! Got it.

Truth be told, I’m a sucker for tall stacks (or long stacks in this case). They do often make for faster solves, since a few crosses and they all fall into place; but I’m still in awe at their construction especially when they are so fresh and well made as today. I have no idea how anyone creates these. So I still tip my hat to the constructor for this fast but fascinating puzzle.

Emus ate my first attempt so apologize if there’s another version which ends up posting.

Fine debut, Coz Berlin. EWE have been published. Quite the complex construction, if not the most difficult solve. The triple triple stacks were fun to see but -- or and -- not hard to fill. Speaking of fill, after filling it, I may need another meal.

Congrats on the debut Coz! I can only imagine how difficult making a triple triple is, but you made it look easy with lively, fun spanners, and minimal glue. Usually a stunt puzzle comes at some expense to the solver, but this was a breezy delight to solve. Thanks!

What an impressive grid.

And what an incredible waste.

The cluing for the long answers—and for the answers in general—was far too simple, and the crosses made them exceptionally easy to get.

With the generally nice fill, this could have been a fantastic puzzle, but it fell flat with mediocre  clues, and a difficulty that was more akin to a Wednesday than a Friday.

What a shame.

4/10.

I had nothing until LEONARDODAVINCI, and the three grid spanning triple stacks looked daunting.  But, the downs were fairly easy, and before long the spanners became obvious. As someone who spent a summer cooking in a Hunanese restaurant, I’m kicking myself for not getting SCALLIONPANCAKE right off the bat.  

Although this turned into a quicker solve than I hoped for, I thoroughly enjoyed it.  The long entries were bright, and there were some nice clues for the shorter fill.  

I was a fledgling baseball fan when Rod CAREW was the model of a great hitter.  He had impeccable bat control and could seemingly spray the ball wherever  he wanted and seldom struck out.  How the game has changed.  Thanks to advent of advanced analytics, most batters swing for the fences, and a much higher percentage of at bats end up as strikeouts, and wining the batting title has become a diminished accomplishment.
Some will say this was too easy for a Friday Puzzle but to me it was fun and who doesn’t need more fun in their lives?
That was easy and hard at the same time. And it had no gimmicks. Hallelujah. Nice puzzle.

Not my fastest Friday ever, but definitely my fastest with that many spanners.

Really liked this one.

Unfortunately I can’t mention any specific clues or fill because of the latest way they’ve managed to botch the Wordplay comments section.

I’d also say I’ll be keeping an eye out for more from this constructor… except I can’t even check the constructor’s name without cancelling my comment and backing out of Wordplay.

For the OGs… what were the prime Wordplay years?

I started around 2022, and I remember loving this forum, even if the EMUs did eat half the posts. Not nearly as easy to follow along or talk about the crossword anymore.
Easy peasy!

Great looking grid, like a barely pulsing ECG or the polygraph of an honest sort! The spanners filled in smoothly thanks to gentle cluing, and I couldn't help but wonder if Leonardo Da Vinci had moist towelettes at hand when he ordered, a la carte, some cadaver ribs to dissect. 

I suppose, having played lead clarinet in our high school marching band, that at one point in my life I stood at the reedy.

It's a good puzzle that has both EWE and EWER. I kept waiting for EWEST to complete the set.

Speaking of this puzzle, on TV tonight I saw LARD and SHAMS as some guy MADE A CLEAN BREAK from reality, liberally applying his special Sauce Americaine to everything he spoke of. No, nothing is sacred: look at the ruins. 

But no more Dr. Doom here. As they say in Dune, "The spice must flow!"

[Does as a group] -- fabulous!
That was a wildly impressive grid, and daunting to look at. Surprisingly easy, but so well done, with straightforward cluing, and no clunkers. Loved it

Okay okay, I say I don't notice or care about grid layout ('design") but it sure was impossible to miss this.  Lots of pretty long space.  And it was all relatively fresh, clever, and junk free to boot.   (Thought for sure the sweet sandwich was yet more oreos, but it wasn't.  Whew.)

Unfortunately it all went pretty fast and easy in the end, as these long spanners always seem to go.  Still a proper Friday challenge, at least at first blush.  At least the spans weren't all obvious to me without a few crosses.

Excellent cluing and such a clean grid. This is one impressive debut. Bravo.
Loved this!

Torn as I am by the ongoing level of difficulty discussion, I'm really not looking forward to the comments tonight. 

I've noticed the phrasing of some of the grand masters here is getting more nuanced, more "between the lines".

I'd rant and rave, but I can't get enough oxygen to do so.  So I better just chill.

By the way, when trying to calm someone down, you say "take a deep breath".  What do you do if the act of taking the deep breath is the very thing that is alarming you?

I'm exaggerating here, but my little Eden is a really, really depressing, and stressful, place at the moment.  It looks like pictures of Pittsburg in the 1950s.  

I'm using fans and furnace filters as makeshift air filters.  I have no idea how well it's working.
At first glance, this grid seemed difficul but it all worked out well.
Amaze! Amaze! Amaze! Fun, not impossible, and so witty. Thank you.

Loved it!  Names worry me, and three up top with two cheek by jowl in the middle made me wonder.  But I grew up in Minneapolis in the ‘60s and was glad for the W confirmation.  Impressive grid for a debut.

To quote Nelson Mandela, "It always seems impossible until it's done." Looking at the practically empty grid, I thought I'd have to give up at some point, or at least do a "check puzzle." Much to my surprise, it got neatly filled in, in thirds, from the bottom up. I had such a good time that I even took a little break to listen to "Anatevka" from Fiddler on the Roof. I can't see or hear anything that sounds akin to "a little bit of this, a little bit of that" without thinking of that song. https://youtu.be/RWiRetxeviw?is=JT7javlouCn6vJvO Congratulations, Coz Berlin, on a mighty fine debut, and thank you. I loved the way you played with the language in your clues, especially [Dated many years ago?] for OLDE. Big chef's kiss for this foodie puzzle!
Its bark was worse than its bite, but that was a daunting first few minutes! I found “Does as a group?” particularly cute. Really fun puzzle!

Flew through this one, but was very enjoyable!

"Hm, I never realized PEWS came in fours..." (ERGH seemed reasonable too)

...they don't. So close to my personal best.

ARGH! 

=)
Immediately got Scallion Pancakes with no crossings. Wavelength. Phenomenal puzzle!

Someone explain how small amounts leads to "mites" (I mean, they are small, but not amounts). What am I missing? 

Tricky puzzle. I had "antiwar lovefest" but then fest didn't really pan out.

Legions of spanners are always intimidating, but I bravely faced all nine of them.  Dodgy prop clues, but straightforward, if you let your mind roll on, and they dropped in, one after another—voila, the spanners became the easiest entries in the puzzle.

Coz Berlin, this was a charmer—funny, thoughtful, and just off enough to slow me down and make me think.  Thank you, and congratulations on your debut.

I can't remember the last time I got a gold star for a Friday. I don't even try to attempt Friday/Saturday puzzles very often. Lucky to have picked a day with an easy one to give it a shot. :) I solved the Friday 7/17/2026 New York Times Daily Crossword in 21:15! https://nytimes.onelink.me/IM32/7owuagxr

When I saw the three sets of spanners, I thought I was done for. I went across first and my grid remained empty for a bit, but then the gimme of LEONARDO DA VINCI opened up the middle of the grid for me. In the end only the top third gave me trouble, but I handled even that, without outside help, completing the puzzle in Wednesday time.

"Does as a group" was a cool clue.

SMOREs are unknown over here. I've never had one. Apparently they're delicious so maybe I'll get to try one some day... Marshmallows only arrived in Poland after 1989, but they're not popular, at all. I remember when I first had them in the early 1990s, having only seen them in American shoes before. They were OK.

Of course Poles love their campfires. We roast kiełbasa over the flames, and we bake potatoes and apples in the cinders and hot ash. In the 80s and 90s my parents and I always had a huge campfire at my grandma's allotment in the spring, when we burned twigs and branches after trimming all the apple trees and berry bushes. These days, with many people being aware of climate change and air pollution, the practice is understandably less widespread.

Only 33 comments 1.5 hours after drop. I’ll take that as a good sign that the comments are not inundated with too easy. 

But honestly. Who cares. 

This grid was just. Chef’s kiss. 

Nine spanners?!? All of them fun!
-I do need to lookup what American sauce is though. Please tell me it isn’t the ‘special sauce’ on Big Macs and smashburgers.  
-why is ‘will’ italicized in the clue for 64 across? 
-should clue for 41A be accompanimentS?  since towelettes is plural?

Even if they weren’t all fun, this is still amazing construction, I would assume?

I like the added difficulty that each triplet section didn’t connect to the others. My natural solve was top, bottom, then middle. 

This was an awesome grid. I can’t imagine how many many hours this took to build. Part of me is sorry, the solve was so quick. Editors could have turned this into a monster if they wanted given how separate each section was. 

Thank you Coz!

I was an American in France who for some reason thought it was sauce armoricaine.  When I google armoricaine now all the AI and hits come back in French.

Eventually both americaine and armoricaine seem acceptable, according to google.  But in my 36 years back in the USA, in contradiction to the Chuck Berry song, haven’t seen a quenelle to pour the sauce on.
Absolutely brilliant construction. I remember the last triple triple (January 18, 2025) like it was yesterday. These constructions remain in my memory for years to come, truly a work of art.

Okay. I just posted my thoughts in the puzzle. 
Now I need technical help. 

I solve on app using iPhone. 
I completed the solve with no help, but when I entered last cell, it gave me a blue star. I thought that was weird. 

I exit the puzzle and every time I re-enter the ER in ERODED are blank. I fill the two letters in, and I get the blue star. I exit. Rinse and repeat. What’s up?  

Am I actually technically alone on this?  No one else experiencing this?  

I didn’t get any rubiks cube animation. Or the animation the week before.  No big deal. But one is troubling.  

Help?
Everybody always love the easy ones.
Fast and fun

I hope Captain Q isn't traumatized by seeing SAUCE L'AMERICAINE in the grid.
I was worried when I saw the three sets of triple spanners, but they turned out not to be fearsome.  They seemed very in the language and the food oriented-ness of some of them and other clues was very helpful to me. 
I'll have some Chinese spareribs to go along with the SCALLION PANCAKE. I ORDERED ALA CARTE and requested some MOIST TOWELETTES be included with the order.
A very gently clued Friday construction. I did like the Does/DEER wordplay.
Congratulations on publication, Coz Berlin.

Coz Berlin’s debut is a no-trouble treble triple. A no-Natick hat-trick. A neat feat, and a total treat. Even the constructor’s notes gets my vote. 

Coz daringly, dangerously dangles an email address and invites us to send the “harshest criticism”. Well, prepare the inbox for an influx of plaudits from the pundits — and crickets from the critics! 

A gem.

Could someone please explain how the answer to 10 down is ANA? Which continents end in ANA? Surely this is an error? Or am I missing something?

So confused!!

First...lovely grid with a delightful triple triple.  I have discovered there is a snapshot esthetic element to grids completely separate to clueing.  I know...most of you already know that.  I can be slow on the uptake.

Second...Is there a course for Times editors at the college they attend called Crossword Editing 303:  The Thin Line Between Pandering to Subscribers and Defanging Setters?

If there is, they need a refresher.

But probably not.  They likely learn on the job.

Such a beautiful feat of construction neutered by the newspaper.

A tough Tuesday themeless which should have been a glorious Friday journey of discovery.
Captivating! hard enough for a Friday but doable in one sit, lots of aha! moments, no obscure entries. Totally enjoyed it!

Wow! Well done (which is NOT how I eat my steak!) Mr. Berlin! As a former chef, now a third grade teacher, I am both a consummate foodie and a bonafide work geek. The real truth is, once a chef, always a chef; it's not like I was decommissioned!

I'm currently on my month-long annual summer pilgrimage to France, homeland of my spouse. My home away from home is satisfying all my culinary senses and desires, and the NYT puzzle is keeping my word nerdiness sated.

I look forward to more from Mr. Berlin.

P.S. The first scanner jumped out at me in a fraction of a second, and I fairly galloped through this delightful puzzle.
Very fun puzzle! Fridays are usually out of my league, but this one was a blast. Big fan of the doe/deer wordplay. Great work Coz, can’t wait to see more from you!!
The impact when it opens and you see the grid was the best part. A real "aha" moment.

Not a fan of clues like 3D, they read like ads to me. 
Otherwise it was a fun puzzle.
Two little lookups--the Fiji neighbor and the Disney lion--and one and done. All those long spanners working out is quite an accomplishment, I think.
Bravo, Coz! What a dazzling stack of stacks! The gentle cluing was a sweet gift. I’m sure tomorrow’s will be tougher.

My first thoughts seeing that expanse of white was ‘Help’. I didn’t start well, as the Chinese food item is unknown to me. I’m reading it as an onion pancake? Sounds, um, unpleasant.
Breathed a sigh of relief with LEONARDO, but I confess, it was a tough gig for me, perhaps because it was Gin Club last night so am feeling just a MITE hazy today.

My absolute favourite clue was the Does in a herd. Laughed like a drain at that one.

A thoroughly enjoyable Friday fill, well done on your debut Mr Berlin.
Love love. Brilliant construction and the nine long answers were so much fun.

"pulled off a triple stack three times, resulting in the triple triple stack: a hat trick of hat tricks. For the folks counting at home, that’s nine spanners gifted to us today, five of which are debuts."

//turns on autocheck resignedly
34D [First name of the singer dubbed the "King of Soul"] Here's OTIS again, singing "My Girl" "I've got sunshine on a cloudy day..." https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0iPtG_O8w8g

Thank you Coz for a delightful triple stack, 9 spanner puzzle to start my weekend. Who would think to add 'moist towelettes' to a crossword puzzle. Also loved 'Is nothing sacred' and 'Make a clean Break'. Liked the Leonardo Davinci' gimme in the middle of the puzzle (that doesn't happen often for me) and of course the Aha moment for the doer/deer clue!

If the verticals were not so easy, then DEFINITELY the triple stacked horizontals would have been a real challenge.....but....
it is not too difficult to complete a long answer, when over half the letters are filled in. Unique, but manageable.

Great crossword otherwise, but one of my pet peeves about modern crosswords is that they include product placements, usually for Lyft, Uber, or Amazon. I see no reason why a mention of an AI-powered Google feature should be allowed anywhere near a crossword, unless money is changing hands, in which case all I can do is throw up my own
What a fun puzzle! I loved the triple stacks.
Delighted to see Otis Day and the Knights mentioned today

Did they make a mix up and publish the easy mode clues in the regular puzzle? I know people get annoyed when we complain that late week puzzles are too easy, but this was my best Friday time by a significant amount and it’s not because I’m getting better at puzzles.
The only mistake I made was doing this on an empty stomach....  Otherwise, was another fun Friday solve!

How I love love love opening 
a themeless grid.

That, “Ooh…what have we here…” vibe.

Coz, it was a beaut. 
And a de-beaut to boot!

A really fun romp.

Thank you!

One more thing-

In Sweden, when we want to buy
“Russian”  dressing, 
we buy American dressing.

I’ve always wondered if Russians
are familiar with it, and do they call it

Swedish dressing?

Spanish sauce?

Danish drizzle?

@Sean – Just for the record, one of the 24 triple-triple stacks in the Times was not “three grid spanning entries stacked on top of one another”, and that was the puzzle of 1/10/15 by Joe Krozel, where two triple-stacks were vertical, on the east and west sides, and the bottom stack was horizontal, giving the three stacks a “U” shape.
I was terrified when I saw the grid, but then the down answers starting coming, and it all came together. Fun.
I seem to be in the minority in that I completed the top third first.

Oh, yes, it is ultra-hard enough to make a triple-triple-stack puzzle of any sort, and harder still to make its quality pass the Times puzzle bar. 

And even harder yet to make the nine spanners sing.  Usually several of them are dull, forced in because they were the only words or phrases that could work.  But look at those spanners today, all rich with color and interest, even those MOIST TOWELETTES. Wow!

Hardest of all is to get the stacks’ crossing downs clean; usually there are several or more gag-worthy crosses – but not today, IMO.

A very, very, impressive NYT puzzle, and even more so because it is a debut.

I find it incredible fun during a solve to fill in a spanner with as few crosses as possible, and today there were nine such opportunities.  Your puzzle, Coz, was like a playground, and I felt like a kid, again and again, “Hah!” after “Hah!”  Congratulations on your debut, sir, and thank you for a most splendid outing!

Oooh, that was fun!  Beautifully constructed, nicely clued, and smooth to solve.  Bravo!

Y’all be kind and stay cool today!
Very much enjoyed the novel fill.  Well done!

Bad clue 

4 out of 7 continents end in -ica. Not one single continent ends in -ana.

I always love when I get stumped by a clue that's more of a trivia question than a brainteasing riddle & this puzzle was full of them. The triple-triple wouldn't have been nearly as fun if these weren't some satisfying nuts to crack on the cross and fill as well. Very satisfying Friday & I hope I see "by Coz Berlin" on another crossword soon!

I like the smartphone feature that adds a watermark to my selfies, saying ASIS.



Loved the cluing today!

I despaired when I first saw the grid with its lack of black squares and began to curse cruel fate when I finished my first pass through the Across clues and had only completed a handful of short entries.

But my spirits improved when I went through the Down clues and completed a goodly number, leading me to be able to figure out the top third of the entire puzzle.

In the end, I solved the entire thing in 9:25 (admittedly, after using Check Puzzle a few times, but I almost always do), only about fifteen seconds over my best time ever for a Friday and found the whole thing very enjoyable.

Now, if I just didn’t have to WORK today…

Forgot to mention earlier that I did like that little nod to the World Cup by cluing KEIRA Knightley to 2002's Bend It Like Beckham, which also starred Parminder Nagra. Here's a trailer for the movie, which reminds me that the movie might merit a rewatch. https://youtu.be/EyorDLWW1Nw?si=z5RNxMV74g60dQFv
Wonderful puzzle and a fun solve

What an imposing grid! All that white space, all those spanners.

But the fill flowed in smoothly, mostly from working the downs.

Thanks, Coz.
I am always amazed by triple stacks. This puzzle was remarkable! An enjoyable solve!

I thought that this was a great Friday puzzle!

First, we are greeted with the beautiful grid, and its amazing sets of spanners.  That's a great way to begin.

Then, there's the solve.  For me, starting from the top, it was slow going at first.  That's not a complaint.  That's what I look forward to on a Friday.

Moving to the bottom, things got a bit easier, but still nothing was certain.  I had to put in guesses without the confirmation of crosses, because the crosses involved those un-guessable spanners.
But with a few crosses in, the spanners became clearer.  I especially loved IS NOTHING SACRED?  Lovely phrase with much resonance for me.

But each area provided new challenges.  Would I have to look up that Marvel villain?  No! LEONARDO to the rescue.

And I am grateful for the proper nouns in this puzzle.  Thank you, LEONARDO; and you, too, Rod CAREW.  Not to mention OTIS Redding and KATO Kaelin.  Saviors all.

Beautiful, fresh, colloquial spanners.  Fun solving experience.  Not too difficult, but not too easy either (for this solver).  Right in the Goldilocks zone.

Thanks, Coz Berlin!  Truly, this is an amazing debut.  I look forward to seeing more from you!
Quick solve but fun nonetheless.

Given that so many folks seem to be enjoying Friday puzzles recently, I’d like to recommend another Friday puzzle from the archives that I came across yesterday: https://www.nytimes.com/crosswords/game/daily/2011/07/01

Enjoyable! I solved the top third, then the bottom third, some of the middle took a while. Had MESA instead of DUNE for too long. SETUP took ages b/c I tried to think of a single word that fit (d’oh!)

When I saw the grid, my heart sank, wondering how I would ever solve this. But I started with the top third, ampnd the Down entries fell in placeenough for me to solve those spanners! And such witty and accessible clues. 

I usually find that I need to solve from the bottom up, but I solved this puzzle too-down in my personal best time for a Friday, less than half my typical time.

More like this, please! Great clues, fun answers, and not requiring esoteric trivia.
Bravo! Five plus years of solving, and I can’t recall a more flawless puzzle.

That was fun. Love the stacks!

Wouldn't [Put into play?] be a good clue for CAST?

A lot of empty boxes on the first few passes, but once I got cooking things fell into place nicely. A triple triple is a wonder to behold and Coz can be proud of this achievement. Even if every clue was Monday-level, this puzzle would stand out as a testament to the creative art of construction. Excellent job Coz -- ya done good 👍
I was surprised how quickly I figured out the grid spanners (not my usual experience on Fridays and Saturdays), thanks to friendly Down entries. Nicely done, Coz!
I thought everyone would be complaining about this being too easy for Friday. Instead I find a praise-fest for its aesthetics. I'm not complaining; I'll take a breezy Friday.
While very easy, this puzzle was a thing of beauty. 'Nuff said.

Impressive feat of construction and a nice challenging workout. Actually surprised that so many found this unusually easy - guess I'm revealing my ignorance. Two of the grid spanners were completely unfamiliar terms and a couple of others were never going to dawn on me just from the clues. No big deal - that's just me. And... puzzle find today - a Wednesday from March 11, 1998 by Bette Sue Cohen. Four 15 letter grid-spanning answers: LEONARDODAVINCI LUIGIEPIRANDELLO ARTUROTOSCANINI VINCENZOBELLINI Just thought it was pretty impressive to come up with those answers and fit them all into the same puzzle. Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=3/11/1998&h=20a Hasta manana. ....
Bad grid (basically three stacked puzzles) and far too easy. Would've been a decent puzzle running on a Wednesday.

Gorgeous stacks!  I always wonder how on earth a constructor can devise these?  It would seem impossible without an enormous amount of crosswordese and other junk, and this puzzle is completely free of all of that.  Kudos.

It was challenging and fun to solve -- harder than recent Fridays have been and I welcomed that.  Although it feels like a century since I last watched baseball, Rod CAREW was an OLDE-time name that popped immediately into my head.  That was a big help in the top stack.

Are they still using LARD in pie crusts?  Isn't it unhealthy?

KATO of the O.J. trial.  Exhibit A in the "how quickly we forget" pantheon.  But it came back to me and now I can't unsee his flowing blond mane.  (He did have a flowing blond mane, didn't he?)

I needed all the crosses for PAWS (54A).  Is that a funny way to clue it or what?

I loved the pitch-perfect clue for IS NOTHING SACRED?  One of the fairest clues I've ever seen.

And a really good clue for our old friend SNL.  It looks brand-new.

Stacks are usually fun to solve and this was especially so.  I'm sure we'd see even more of them if they weren't so darned hard to make.

Like having some potato chips. A nice snack. Nine spanners really didn’t mean anything, though. The crosses made it a cinch. Oh well.

I do admire constructors who can put stacks together this way, though. It must take a lot of work, assuming that they aren’t using AI/computer assistance.

Felt like a Sunday, and I mean that in a very good way.  While not jumbo-sized, the triple triple made it seem so, and, for me, the 15-letter answers kept falling into place, one by one.

I enjoyed solving this.

At first glance, with the triple sets of spanning stacks, I thought this puzzle would be impossible.  Instead, I realized that it was very approachable and enjoyable.  The vertical fills gave me just enough letters to deduce the spanners .
Very nice feat of construction.
Maybe slightly easier than the average Friday, but I am not complaining.
This was a good one!! Thankful for the crosses to help with the wonderful spanners. Lower left gave me a fit because I stuck with “sicem” for too long. Nicely done! Happy Friday, y’all!
Zippity doo dah. Personal best for a Friday (15:33), almost 1/2 my average. Congrats to the constructor for this fabulous and fun Times debut.

Except for the Axis of Evil (little quadrant bounded by 24D to 26D on the top and 44A at the bottom) this was going smoothly.  Giving up a tomato/vinegar-based BBQ sauce was so difficult!  LEONARDO DA VINCI stuck his nose in and messed things up.  ANTIWAR Marchers created havoc--send in the ICEE Thugs!

TUVALU?  
I wanted GAIL at 34D and she did not go quietly...
Rod CAREW, eh?  Not Hammerin' Hank AARON?  That seems wrong in several ways!
And re pie crust:
What is it with the LARD all the time?  Ugh, ugh, ugh.  At least use a "vegetable shortening" (with some butter :0), mayhap?)
Extremely satisfying!

At first my heart sank when I didn't know the first long one nor hardly any of the down clues, so I just revealed the PANCAKE in order to get started. (Scallions = spring onions in England and sybies in Scotland).
Then SWATCH helped me to put in WAR but the TOWELETTES defeated me, whether dry or MOIST. But then the sauce .... nope.
Some of the downs were ok , remembering SKOR, SIC ON and SCAR from before.
But Heath ("open, desolate moorland") is the name of a candy bar? Did they not know their Macbeth?

     Where the place?
     Upon the heath.
     There to meet with Macbeth
     Fair is foul, and foul is fair.
     Hover through the foul and filthy air.
I'm feeling dumb, but... why was it "ana" instead of "ica" for the ends of most continents?

Wow I even beat the Wordplay column today.
Tell you the truth, I’m a sucker for tall stacks (long stacks in this case). Fact is, they often make for fast solves, since you get a few crosses and they all fall into place. That was the case today. But I’m still in awe when they are all fresh and interesting, like today, and I have no idea how they get constructed so well. So I’ll take the fast but fascinating puzzle today and tip my hat regardless.

Loved it! Congratulations, Coz, on a fine debut. At first I thought I'd be done for with so many spanners, but, as others have pointed out, they helped me quite a bit with the downs.

I can't imagine the time it took to construct this beauty -- hope to see more from you soon, Coz!😊
Slowly but surely
For a little while, I was trying to figure out what kind of OMELETTES would be served with ribs.

I peeked at xwordinfo for the photo....COZ is short for ??-- and makes a memorable name-tag.
In his xword notes, COZ asks for suggestions:  my one thought was that the shot was taken with him staring into the sun, which makes it quite difficult to smile.  (I expect he has a nice one.)
Triple Stacks are great! My absolute favorite – thank you Coz!
I was staring so hard at the puzzle for hours, thinking of what went wrong only to realize I had ANTIWARcOVEnaNT the entire time.
Good'un.
I'm gonna say it- I loved this puzzle!!! "Does as a group" was a phenomenal clue! Nine puzzle spanning entries is a total feat!! TGIF!

This was fun. 
Like doing three Midi puzzles.

Big city stacks! Nicely done, Mr Berlin...hard to believe this is a debut. "Unearthed?" for ERODED was nice and devious.

I was unfamiliar with SAUCE AMERICAINE, as I don't eat seafood, and certainly not lobster. I thought perhaps it was a Gallic cheap shot about putting ketchup on everything...or ranch dressing. Would you put Hollandaise on seafood? Certainly on my eggs Benedict. Never mind, it doesn't fit.

A perfect paradigm for a themeless Wednesday puzzle. Very nicely constructed, but with nine easily ascertained horizontal spanners (and no lengthy vertical entries) this seemed too easy for a Friday. Done with no help in 14 minutes, and I'm not a fast solver or even a competent typist.

Congrats on the debut in any case.

When fires were making our lives miserable here, we kept the doors and windows closed and the air purifiers (one upstairs, one down) on round the clock.  

The worst fire here was the Oakland Hills Firestorm (also known as the Tunnel Fire) of October 1991. It burned 1,520 acres in the Oakland and Berkeley hills, destroying 3,469 homes and apartment units, causing 25 fatalities, and resulting in an estimated $1.5 billion in damages.
